topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the familiar lines of the worn paperback, his thumb lingering on the page where his beloved protagonist's defining moment resided. A cold prickle started at the base of his neck, spreading outward. He reread the passage, the words suddenly feeling alien, hollowed out. He flipped to the source material, the author’s published collection, and the resemblance hit him like a physical blow. A sharp intake of breath caught in his throat, and his hand trembled so violently he had to set the book down on the mahogany desk. He stood up, pacing the length of his study, his movements jerky and agitated. Every shadow in the room seemed to shift, and the silence of the apartment amplified the frantic thumping in his chest. He needed air, needed distance, but his feet felt rooted to the expensive Persian rug.

[story 2]

"It’s… impossible," Clara whispered, her voice thin and reedy. She stared at the manuscript laid out before her—her own meticulously crafted narrative, now juxtaposed with the text from the celebrated novelist. The similarities were too precise, the structural echoes too undeniable. A bead of sweat traced a slow path down her temple. She picked up a pen, intending to cross out a section, but her hand shook too much for the precise correction she desired. She leaned back in her chair, the leather groaning under her weight, and closed her eyes, trying to steady the rapid flutter beneath her ribs. When she opened them, the room seemed too bright, the air too thick. She felt a profound sense of disorientation, as if the very foundations of the story she had built were beginning to crumble beneath her gaze.

[story 3]

Marcus kept glancing over his shoulder, expecting someone to materialize in the hallway. The silence of the gallery felt predatory. He held the printed excerpts, the ink seeming to mock him with its familiar phrasing. He tried to focus on the abstract art on the wall, but his vision kept snagging on the copied sentences. He walked to the window, pulling the heavy velvet curtain aside just an inch, letting a sliver of the outside world flood the room. His jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath his skin. He took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to regulate the erratic rhythm of his heart. Every muscle in his body felt coiled, ready for a sudden, unwelcome movement. He didn't want to look at the text again; the air around it felt charged, vibrating with something deeply wrong.
